title: Rocket Lab and Firefly Get Upgrades. Why the Space Selloff Was ‘Unwarranted.’
content:
Shares of  Rocket Lab  and  Firefly Aerospace  advanced Monday after the space stocks received upgrades following the sharp sector selloff late last week on Elon Musk’s  SpaceX  initial public offering debut.  KeyBanc Capital Markets analyst Michael Leshok on Sunday upgraded both Rocket Lab and Firefly to Overweight (Buy) from the previous Sector Weight (Hold), setting price targets of $135 and $50, respectively, for the space stocks.  Rocket Lab rose 6.7% to $109.25 on Monday, clawing back some of its 11% drop from Friday.

Continue Reading

title: KeyBanc Upgrades Rocket Lab and Firefly After SpaceX IPO Selloff
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) rose 4.77% intraday and Firefly Aerospace (NASDAQ:FLY) gained 6.90% after KeyBanc upgraded both companies to Overweight, with Rocket Lab receiving a $135 price target implying roughly 35% upside from current levels. The upgrades follow a sharp selloff on Friday, when Firefly dropped 19% and Rocket Lab fell 10% as investors rotated into SpaceX (NASDAQ:SPCX) on its record IPO debut day.

KeyBanc cited Rocket Lab's position as the second-largest player in the commercial space launch market, with its Electron rocket completing approximately 90 successful missions. The firm said it gained additional visibility on the Neutron rocket program over the past six months, with a January testing anomaly fully resolved and the first launch still on track for later this year. KeyBanc noted Rocket Lab's combined satellite design, manufacturing, and launch capabilities position it to adapt to market changes and potentially build its own satellite constellation.

The upgrades come as the SpaceX IPO sparks renewed interest in the broader space economy. Rocket Lab is also set to join the Nasdaq-100 on June 22, a move that will trigger passive fund buying.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 4 Warning Signs with PLTR. Is RKLB fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

View Comments

title: Rocket Lab Stock Jumps on Major Analyst Upgrade
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) shares jumped 8% on Monday after KeyBanc lifted its rating on the space company and Firefly Aerospace (NASDAQ:FLY) to Overweight from Sector Weight, saying the commercial launch market still has room for several winners despite the debut of SpaceX (NASDAQ:SPCX), according to a Monday KeyBanc research note.

The broker set a $135 target for Rocket Lab and a $50 target for Firefly. It said the recent selloff in space names after SpaceX's IPO looked more like portfolio rebalancing than a shift in the industry outlook.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 10 Warning Signs with DOW. Is RKLB fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

KeyBanc said satellite demand, limited launch capacity, higher U.S. defense spending and NASA activity may keep the market tight for years. It also argued SpaceX's Starship program may absorb much of its own future lift capacity, leaving room for rivals.

For Rocket Lab, KeyBanc pointed to its Electron rocket, planned Neutron vehicle and $2.2 billion backlog. For Firefly, it cited the company's lunar work, a recent $75 million NASA MoonFall contract and its SciTec software unit.

title: Stocks making the biggest moves midday: SpaceX, Roku, Tripadvisor, Ferrari & more
content: Check out the companies making the biggest moves in midday trading: SpaceX — Shares were up 9% in the company's second day of trading on Monday. SpaceX in its debut at the Nasdaq on Friday rose 19% from its IPO price of $135 per share. The company raised a total of $85.7 billion including the underwriters' "greenshoe" overallotment. Ferrari — The luxury carmaker's stock jumped 5% after Morgan Stanley upgraded it to overweight from equal weight with a price target of $438. Analysts said Ferrari's decline over the past year was due to value compression, not earnings or brand deterioration. Further, dealer visits in the U.S. and Europe show demand remains strong for its special series and collectible models. Tripadvisor — The travel company's stock rose nearly 7% after it announced the sale of its European online restaurant reservation platform to American Express . Activist investor Starboard had been pushing Tripadvisor to sell The Fork and to explore strategic options for the entire company. American Express, which also owns Resy, saw its stock rise 4%. Generac Holdings — Shares of the power generator company rose 2% after announced plans to acquire a new facility in Belvidere, Illinois. The deal will help the company expand its packaging capacity for large-megawatt power generators. Energy stocks —The group tumbled as U.S. oil prices fell 5% to around $80 per barrel following a U.S.-Iran deal that is set to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. APA shares fell more than 5%, while Devon Energy shed almost 2%. Marathon Petroleum tumbled more than 4%, and EOG Resources was down close to 3%. Chevron and Exxon Mobil were also falling about 4%. Airlines and cruise stocks — Beneficiaries of lower fuel prices rose on Monday. United Airlines was up more than 5%, while Delta Air Lines rose 2%. Norwegian Cruise Line and Carnival Corporation jumped around 4%, while Royal Caribbean was up roughly 6%. Fox Corporation , Roku — Fox shares sunk 15% after the company announced it was acquiring Roku for $160 per share, an 11% premium compared with the stock's Friday close. The cash-and-stock transaction will build a company that is at the forefront of sports, news and streaming, Fox said in a press release. Roku Nasdaq-traded shares were down slightly. Micron Technology — The memory and storage solutions company was up more than 9% as technology stocks rose amid the broader market rally and the stock received a price target hike from TD Cowen. Analysts at the firm now forecast the stock hitting $1,500, calling the role of memory in the artificial buildout as structural, not cyclical. Firefly Aerospace , Rocket Lab — The stocks were up on Monday after KeyBanc upgraded both of them to overweight. It came as Firefly and Rocket Lab fell 19% and more than 10%, respectively, on Friday after the SpaceX IPO led investors to move money from other aerospace names into the Elon Musk giant. Firefly and Rocket Lab each rose around 5% on Monday. Freeport-McMoRan — The miner was up more than 1% as gold prices rose more than 3% on Monday. Investors moved back into gold as a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal alleviated worries that the Federal Reserve may have to hike rates in the coming months to combat rising prices due to the conflict. —CNBC's Nick Wells and Christina Cheddar Berk contributed to this report.

title: Stocks making the biggest moves premarket: SpaceX, United Airlines, Roku & more
content: Check out the companies making the biggest moves premarket: SpaceX — Shares were up 5% as the company prepared for it second day of trading on Monday. SpaceX in its debut at the Nasdaq on Friday rose 19% from its IPO price of $135 per share. Energy stocks —The group tumbled as U.S. oil prices fell 5% to near $80 per barrel following a U.S.-Iran deal that is set to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. APA and Devon Energy were both off more than 3.5%. Marathon Petroleum and EOG Resources were down 3%. Chevron and Exxon Mobil were also falling more than 2.5%. Airlines and cruise stocks — Beneficiaries of lower fuel prices rose in premarket trading on Monday. United Airlines was up more than 5%, while Delta Air Lines rose 4%. Norwegian Cruise Line and Carnival Corporation jumped 4.5%, while Royal Caribbean was up 4%. Paramount Skydance — The media conglomerate was up almost 5% after the U.S. Justice Department on Friday announced it approved Paramount's acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery . While the federal hurdle is clear, the company's proposed merger could still see challenges at the state level. Fox Corporation , Roku — Fox shares sunk 12% after the company announced it was acquiring Roku for $160 per share, an 11% premium compared to the stock's Friday close. The cash and stock transaction will build a company that is at the forefront of sports, news and streaming, Fox said in a press release. Roku shares rose 2%. Micron Technology — The memory and storage solutions company was up more than 7.5% as technology stocks rose amid the broader market rally and the stock received a price target hike from TD Cowen. Analysts at the firm now forecast the stock hitting $1,500, calling the role of memory in the artificial buildout as structural, not cyclical. Firefly Aerospace , Rocket Lab — The stocks were up on Monday after KeyBanc upgraded both of them to overweight. It came as Firefly and Rocket Lab fell 19% and 10%, respectively, on Friday after the SpaceX IPO led investors to move money from other aerospace names into the Elon Musk giant. Firefly was up 5% in premarket trading Monday, while Rocket Lab rose nearly 4%. Freeport-McMoRan — The miner was up 4% as gold prices rose more than 2.5% on Monday. Investors moved back into gold as a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal alleviated worries that the Federal Reserve may have to hike rates in the coming months to combat rising prices due to the conflict.

title: These 2 space stocks were upgraded at KeyBanc today
content:
Investing.com -- KeyBanc Capital Markets upgraded two space stocks to Overweight from Sector Weight in a note on Monday, arguing that recent sector-wide volatility tied to SpaceX's record-setting IPO has created compelling entry points across commercial space names.

Rocket Lab and Firefly Aerospace are the stocks in question.

Analyst Michael Leshock set a $135 price target on Rocket Lab, reflecting roughly 40% upside, calling the company "the clear #2 to SPCX" with the potential to "achieve a similar growth trajectory to SPCX over time."

KeyBanc noted Rocket Lab's backlog stood at more than $2.2 billion as of the first quarter, up 20% sequentially and more than 100% year-over-year, with a potential $700 million Mars Telecommunications Orbiter contract award expected in the second half of 2026.

The firm estimates Rocket Lab is more than 95% vertically integrated on its satellite bus and more than 90% on its Electron rocket.

For Firefly Aerospace, KeyBanc set a $50 price target, implying more than 50% upside. The firm cited Firefly's exposure to NASA's lunar initiatives, noting its Blue Ghost lander is "the only lander from a commercial space company to achieve a fully successful landing on the Moon."

KeyBanc also pointed to the recent $75 million MoonFall NASA contract award and Firefly's SciTec missile tracking software, which "tracked ~1,000 missiles in the first 30 days" of the Iran conflict.

KeyBanc believes the sector selloff around SpaceX's IPO was "unwarranted and largely systematic," driven by funds making room for the new listing rather than any deterioration in fundamentals.

The firm cited accelerating NASA activity, constrained launch supply, and rising defense budgets, including roughly $56 billion for space-based systems in the proposed FY27 defense budget, as durable tailwinds for the sector.

title: NASA Hires Firefly Aerospace to Build a Drone Aircraft Carrier -- to the Moon
content:
NASA's Moon Base plans are shifting into high gear. As part of a wide-ranging "update on Moon Base rovers, landers, missions" last month, NASA announced a novel plan for mapping the Moon's surface -- and it involves Firefly Aerospace (NASDAQ: FLY), the first American company to land a spacecraft on the Moon (upright) in the past 50 years. Here's how it's going to work.Image source: Firefly Aerospace.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »

Project MoonFall

It sounds a bit like a James Bond movie title, but NASA has dubbed its latest lunar project "MoonFall." Launching atop an as-yet-unidentified carrier rocket sometime in 2028, Firefly Aerospace will send one of its Elytra Dark spacecraft (the largest and most capable of the Elytra versions) to the Moon. Its cargo: four 550-pound, 7-foot-wide, 4-foot-tall "hopping" drones built for NASA by CalTech's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL). Thirty miles above the lunar surface, Elytra will deploy its cargo to descend independently to the Moon. (Elytra itself will remain in lunar orbit.)

The four drones will land on the lunar surface at multiple sites. Then they will spend the next 14 days hopping all around the lunar south pole, using their rockets to launch into the "air" and then descend back to the surface. During each hop, the MoonFall drones will use high-definition cameras to map the surface they traverse. Their mission is to develop the highest-resolution map of the lunar surface ever created, detect potential caches of water ice, and confirm potential landing sites for Artemis IV and future crewed missions.

What it means for Firefly

Given all the missions NASA has planned for its MoonFall drones, the price it's paying Firefly to conduct the mission looks bargain-basement cheap -- just $75 million. (Paying JPL to build the drones, and hiring a rocket company to launch them and Elytra to the Moon will add some tens of millions more to the total project cost.)

Firefly gets more out of this mission than just money, however. Scouting landing sites for Artemis IV and future missions, Firefly is making itself integral to Project Artemis. There's great PR value in that (as well as pole position when bidding on further Artemis contracts).

The company is also using MoonFall as an advertisement to remind investors of its success in landing the only (upright) mission to the Moon in the past 50-plus years. As the company points out: "Firefly's Elytra spacecraft are built with proven systems from [the Blue Ghost Mission 1 lunar lander], including the core avionics, carbon composite structures, and Spectre engines that enabled the first successful commercial Moon landing."

Story Continues

Successful completion of the MoonFall mission, combined with a higher profile when bidding on future missions, should give Firefly a leg up as it races to meet analyst forecasts to more than 10x its annual revenue from $160 million last year to more than $2.1 billion by 2030, as estimated by S&P Global Market Intelligence. A growth stock investor couldn't ask for anything more.

Should you buy stock in Firefly Aerospace right now?

Before you buy stock in Firefly Aerospace, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Firefly Aerospace wasn't one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you'd have $433,268!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you'd have $1,259,391!*

Now, it's worth noting Stock Advisor's total average return is 935% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 207% for the S&P 500. Don't miss the latest top 10 list, available with Stock Advisor, and join an investing community built by individual investors for individual investors.

Rich Smith has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.

Key Points

NASA has awarded Firefly $75 million to deliver four "hopping" drones to the Moon. The aerospace company's biggest space vehicle may make its first launch in 2028.10 stocks we like better than Firefly Aerospace ›

title: SA Asks: Space stocks sold off on Friday; should you buy the dip?
content:
[Shot of a Launch Pad Complex: Successful Rocket Launching with Crew on a Space Exploration Mission. Flying Spaceship Blasts Flames and Smoke on a Take-Off. Humanity in Space, Conquering Universe.]
gorodenkoff/iStock via Getty Images

Shares of space and satellite stocks fell significantly Friday in the wake of SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) massive initial public offering. 

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Jack Bowman [https://seekingalpha.com/author/jack-bowman], Julia Ostian [https://seekingalpha.com/author/julia-ostian], and Daniel Jones [https://seekingalpha.com/author/daniel-jones] if investors should buy the dip or stay on the sidelines.

Jack Bowman [https://seekingalpha.com/author/jack-bowman]: My feeling is that the SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) IPO is pulling a lot of money out of these other firms, and I wouldn't be surprised if this pain lasts longer than just the IPO day. 

I wouldn't jump in too quickly. It's probably best to wait a few weeks until after SPCX is in the Russell 1000 and Nasdaq 100 indexes. Until then, I would expect more downward pressure on all the non-SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) space stocks as retail investors continue to pile in, expecting the passive investor pump for SPCX from the index inclusions. 

Investors should also consider how the dynamics of a public SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) change what space stocks retail investors are likely to buy into moving forward. Firms like AST SpaceMobile (ASTS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ASTS]) have been synonymous with the satellite business and may fall out of favor as SpaceX becomes the new single-stock proxy for the satellite business. That's a big risk to some of these, and that's also why I would caution buying in too early, even if this dip seems mostly sentiment-driven.

Julia Ostian: [https://seekingalpha.com/author/julia-ostian] In my last article, I discussed how SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) IPO might attract new investors looking for space alternatives, and I still think it will be correct looking longer-term forward. On Friday, though, it seems investors decided to “free” those space holdings to make funds available for the largest public offering in history. This selloff, in my opinion, is a discount rather than a problem.

I’d be especially excited about stocks like Intuitive Machines (LUNR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LUNR]) as they do not directly compete with SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), benefit from the increased interest in lunar exploration, and could even partner with companies like SpaceX. 

After LUNR, I’d consider buying Firefly Aerospace (FLY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FLY]), another stock that is relatively cheap, especially compared to its peers. 

Even after the drop, Rocket Lab (RKLB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RKLB]) is very expensive, trading close to SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) in valuation, which is problematic since SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) has clearly stronger momentum and investor interest. Even though RKLB is one of the top players in this industry, I’d like it to reprice further before considering buying. 

Daniel Jones [https://seekingalpha.com/author/daniel-jones]: June 12th was a fascinating day for market participants, particularly those invested in space-oriented businesses. On that date, shares of Space Exploration Technologies (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), also known as SpaceX, finally went public, turning Elon Musk into the first trillionaire in history. 

If you follow my work closely, you know that I am incredibly bearish about this prospect. I fully expect shares to plunge as the reality behind fundamentals eventually overwhelms the hype around the company and its potential. Interestingly, however, the best opportunity might not be to take the risky course and short the business so much as it might be to buy some of the companies that took a hit that day. 

It appears as though all or most all space-oriented businesses have experienced declines. There are different ways to interpret this. The fact is, leading up to June 12th, the shares of many of these businesses rose materially. The hope, likely, was that capital would flow into other space-oriented firms as the market validated the opportunity that exists for SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]). But when SpaceX ultimately saw its stock start trading lower than what analysts anticipated, that caused expectations to deflate somewhat.

Personally, I believe that the best player in the industry is probably Iridium Communications (IRDM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IRDM]). It might not be a fast-growing name, but it does see steady growth with strong cash flow margins. Iridium operates a constellation of satellites, and even at a time when SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) is growing, it continues to do a fine job. 

Iridium's stock has climbed 89.9% since I upgraded it from a ‘hold’ to a ‘buy’ in July 2024. The S&P 500, meanwhile, has risen only 31.8% over that time. In my most recent article about Iridium, published in May of this year, I said I thought the stock offered additional upside. 

There could be some other prospects worth considering, but some of these companies are much more speculative, generating significant losses as they continue to scale. Virgin Galactic (SPCE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCE]), as an example, plunged nearly 32% on Friday. But unlike Iridium, it's generated net losses on almost no revenue. I have not written articles about all of these candidates. But the ones that I have written about are firms that I have been largely neutral about at best or bearish about at worst.

In the neutral camp, for instance, we have Rocket Lab (RKLB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RKLB]), which I last wrote about in 2023, and EchoStar (SATS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SATS]), which I called a ‘hold’ in March of this year. There have been others that I have been quite bearish about, with one notable example being AST SpaceMobile (ASTS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ASTS]), which I called a ‘sell’ in October 2025. 

The key, if you are going to invest in companies in this space, is to focus on a strong economic moat and steady and reliable cash flows. That certainly narrows the list of viable candidates down considerably. This strategy, combined with staying away from SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) like the plague, is the best advice that I can impart upon you.

_Daniel Jones is the author of the investment newsletter Crude Value Insights [https://seekingalpha.com/checkout/mp_1209]__._

 	* Top Cable and Satellite Stocks [https://seekingalpha.com/screeners/940837608f-Top-Cable-and-Satellite-Stocks]

title: Trending stocks this week as investors cheer SpaceX IPO, diplomacy hopes
content:
[Wall street sign in New York with New York Stock Exchange background]
naphtalina/iStock via Getty Images

Wall Street finished the week higher, rebounding from an early selloff as investors digested fresh inflation data, monitored developments in U.S.-Iran diplomacy, and welcomed the blockbuster public debut of SpaceX (spcx).

Inflation remained a key focus after data showed the U.S. consumer price index rose 4.2% year over year in May, matching expectations and accelerating from April's 3.8% increase.

Geopolitical developments also supported sentiment. President Donald Trump suggested progress in negotiations with Iran, saying a planned U.S. military operation had been called off as talks moved closer to a potential agreement that could ease tensions across the Middle East.

For the week, the blue-chip Dow (DJI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DJI]) climbed up +0.66%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500 [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SP500]) moved up +0.65%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/COMP:IND]) added +0.70%.

HERE’S WHAT CAUGHT INVESTOR ATTENTION THIS WEEK:

SPACEX'S (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) long-awaited initial public offering happened on Friday, as shares officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, for a gain of 11%. Shares ended the day at $160.95, up 19.2%, after touching a high of $176.52 earlier in the session.

SPACE-RELATED AND SATELLITE STOCKS swung lower on Friday after SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) IPO fired off at a trading level below many of the high-flying expectations of investors. Notable movers included Virgin Galactic (SPCE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCE]) -30%, Rocket Lab (RKLB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RKLB]) -8%, York Space Systems (YSS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/YSS]) -17%, Planet Labs (PL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/PL]) -10%, Firefly Aerospace (FLY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FLY]) -18%, Redwire (RDW [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RDW]) -8%, Satellogic (SATL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SATL]) -11%, EchoStar (SATS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SATS]) -12%, AST SpaceMobile (ASTS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ASTS]) -13%, Sidus Space (SIDU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SIDU]) -14%, BlackSky Technologies (BKSY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BKSY]) -11%, and Intuitive Machines (LUNR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/LUNR]) -11%.

SUPER MICRO COMPUTER (SMCI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SMCI]) fell on Wednesday after the company revealed a series of equity and equity-linked financing transactions totaling $7B to help it fund the purchase of components necessary to complete recent orders for its advanced AI servers. The AI infrastructure solutions provider is offering about 45.45M common shares at $27.50 a piece. It is also offering 75M depositary shares, representing 1/20th interests in a new 7.0% series A mandatory convertible preferred stock, at $50 per depositary share.

INTEL (INTC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/INTC]) surged on Thursday after BofA double upgraded the chipmaker's stock to Buy from Underperform. The firm also raised the price target on the stock to $135 from $96. The company also led chip and AI-related stocks higher on Monday following a report that Google and Nvidia are considering the company as a backup chip manufacturer.

ORACLE (ORCL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ORCL]) fell even after the IT giant reported fiscal fourth-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street's forecast. For the period ending May 31, Oracle said it earned an adjusted $2.11 per share as revenue rose 21% year-over-year to $19.18B. Analysts had expected adjusted earnings of $1.97 per share on $19.09B in revenue. Total remaining performance obligations grew $85B during the period to $638B.

CORNING (GLW [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GLW]) shares jumped after Amazon (AMZN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMZN]) announced a multibillion-dollar partnership with the company to expand U.S.-based fiber-optics manufacturing. The deal is aimed at supporting Amazon's growing AI and cloud data center infrastructure.

ADOBE (ADBE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ADBE]) shares fell even as the Photoshop maker reported fiscal second-quarter results and guidance that topped estimates. For the period ending May 29, Adobe said it earned an adjusted $5.96 per share as revenue rose 13% year-over-year to $6.62B. Analysts had expected the company to earn $5.81 per share on $6.45B in revenue. Total annualized recurring revenue exiting the quarter was $27.10B, Adobe added.

S&P Dow Jones Indices announced that MARVELL TECHNOLOGY (MRVL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MRVL]), a semiconductor company benefiting from AI-related demand, and FLEX (FLEX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FLEX]), an electronics manufacturing company, will join the S&P 500 as part of its quarterly rebalancing. They will replace Pool Corp. (POOL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/POOL]) and Campbell's (CPB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CPB]), which are being moved out of the index.

SALESFORCE (CRM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CRM]) slipped even as it doubled down on AI-led monetization. The company signed a definitive agreement on Monday to acquire m3ter, a metering and rating platform focused on consumption-based monetization. The acquisition is expected to close in Q2 FY2027, depending on approvals.

AMAZON (AMZN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMZN]) announced on Wednesday that its supply chain services business has expanded its less-than-truckload freight service in the U.S. so that any business can now ship palletized freight to a wide range of destinations, not just into Amazon (AMZN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMZN]) facilities. The offer is aimed at giving companies of all sizes more flexible and cost-efficient freight options by letting them share trailer space rather than paying for full truckloads.
